1.exposition: Paul cannot say a simple hello. The greeting that opens this letter is the longest in all his correspondence, and every clause of it is theology: before he has named a single reader he has compressed the whole gospel into his own self-introduction. He is a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God — a gospel that is no novelty but the fulfillment of what God promised beforehand through his prophets (v.2). Its content is God’s own Son, descended from David according to the flesh and declared to be the Son of God with power by the resurrection from the dead. Through him Paul received grace and apostleship to bring the obedience of faith among all the nations (v.5), and among them stand the Roman believers, beloved of God and called to be saints. The letter’s whole burden is sounded in its very first breath.
1.original_language: Three Greek words set the tone. Paul is a doulos (“bondservant, slave”), wholly owned by his Master; an apostolos (“one sent”) by commission, not self-appointment; and aphōrismenos (“separated, marked off”) for the gospel, the same verb used of his call in Acts 9:15. In v.4 the crucial verb is horizō (“to mark out, define, appoint”): the resurrection did not make Jesus the Son but publicly marked him out as what he eternally was. And the goal of his commission is hypakoē pisteōs (v.5) — the obedience that consists in, and flows from, faith itself.
1.historical_context: Paul writes to a congregation he neither founded nor has visited, so he stretches the standard Greco-Roman letter opening (sender, recipient, greeting) across seven verses, letting his theology do the work of introduction before he arrives (cf. Romans 15:23–24). “Servant” carries a double resonance: in Rome a high household slave of a great lord could wield real authority, while “servant of the Lord” was the honored title of Israel’s prophets. The messianic credential “of the seed of David” (v.3) reaches back to the promise that David’s heir would reign forever (2 Samuel 7:12–16).
1.christ: The compact confession of vv.3–4 holds two truths in one breath: the Son is David’s true heir “according to the flesh,” and he was “declared to be the Son of God with power… by the resurrection.” This is not adoption — Jesus did not become Son at Easter — but enthronement: he was openly designated in power to be what he always was, his sonship publicly installed as the psalm foretold (Psalms 2:7; cf. Acts 13:33). And grace and peace flow jointly “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ” (v.7) — Son named beside Father as one fountain of blessing, an unforced witness to his deity.

verses.note: Paul names himself three ways at once: a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God. grammatical-historical Robertson presses doulos — not a hired hand but a bondservant, wholly owned by his Lord, so that lowliness and dignity meet in one title. eastern Chrysostom hears the humility of it; yet the office is no self-promotion, for Paul is an apostle by divine call, not his own choosing (Galatians 1:1). The three words together set his whole identity within Christ and the gospel.

The gospel is promised afore by his prophets in the holy scriptures. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the apologetic force: what Paul preaches is no innovation but the long-foretold fulfillment of the Old Testament hope. reformed Calvin adds that the gospel is “of God” — its author and origin divine — so that prophet and apostle proclaim one continuous word, the Testaments bound together.

The gospel’s subject is a person: his Son… made of the seed of David according to the flesh. reformed Calvin says the whole gospel is contained in Christ, so that whoever removes a single step from him departs from it. grammatical-historical Robertson notes that “according to the flesh” quietly implies a nature besides the human — the phrase would be idle of a mere man — while the Davidic descent fulfills the ancient promise of the Messiah-King (2 Samuel 7:12–16).

And declared to be the Son of God with power… by the resurrection from the dead. The witnesses insist on the verb: grammatical-historical Robertson reads “declared” (horizō) as marked out, defined, proved, the rising giving God’s seal to the Son “in power”; reformed Gill sharpens it — not made the Son but manifested so. “The spirit of holiness” stands in antithesis to “the flesh” of v.3, naming the higher nature in which that sonship resided, publicly vindicated when he rose (cf. Acts 13:33).

Through Christ, Paul says, we have received grace and apostleship, for obedience to the faith among all nations. grammatical-historical Ellicott distinguishes the two gifts — grace, the favour Paul shares with every Christian; apostleship, the office peculiar to him. reformed Calvin reads “the obedience of faith” as the obedience which faith itself is: the chief act of obedience to God is to believe him, the chief disobedience unbelief. Its scope is “all nations,” the whole Gentile world (cf. Acts 9:15), and its end the honor of Christ’s name.

“Among whom are ye also the called of Jesus Christ.” reformed Calvin presses “called”: not merely those to whom the gospel is addressed, but those effectually called and made partakers of Christ. eastern Theodoret hears the mercy — the Romans, once among the nations in darkness, now belong to Christ, gathered into the very harvest of the Gentiles Paul was sent to reap.

The address at last: to all that be in Rome, beloved of God, called to be saints. reformed Calvin roots their whole standing in God’s prior love — the calling flows from being beloved, not the reverse. Then the apostolic blessing, “grace to you and peace,” grace the free source and peace its fruit; and it comes “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ” together, the Son set beside the Father as one origin of divine blessing, a quiet witness to his deity.

witnesses.point: The threefold self-designation — servant, apostle, set apart — joins deep humility to real authority: a servant, yet the servant of so great a Lord.
“Servant” is doulos, a bondservant wholly owned by Christ; and “declared” (horizō) means the resurrection marked Jesus out as God’s Son in power, not made him so.
The whole gospel is included in Christ; grace and peace flow from Father and Son alike, the Son named beside the Father as the fountain of blessing.
The Romans, once Gentiles in darkness, are now the called of Jesus Christ, gathered into the harvest of the nations Paul was sent to reap.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Reads vv.3–4 as an early, probably pre-Pauline confession whose two stages — the Son as David’s seed, then the Son enthroned in power by resurrection — Paul takes up as a summary of the gospel he serves.

1.application: Paul’s identity is defined by Christ before it is defined by any task: he is a servant owned by his Lord, and only then an apostle sent by him. The same order holds for every believer — calling flows from belonging, and service from being “beloved of God.” Rest your standing there before you ever measure it by what you do.

8.exposition: From greeting Paul turns to thanksgiving, and even his gratitude carries the argument forward. He thanks God through Christ that the Romans’ faith is spoken of throughout the whole world, and calls God to witness that he prays for them without ceasing (v.9), longing at last to visit. Yet no sooner does he offer to impart some spiritual gift than he softens it: they will be comforted together by their mutual faith. He has often purposed to come and been hindered (v.13); as a debtor both to Greeks and to barbarians he is ready to preach the gospel even at Rome. Affection and mission press together toward the thesis that follows.
8.historical_context: Thanksgiving formulas were conventional in ancient letters, but Paul fills the form with theology (v.8). That a church’s faith could be “spoken of throughout the whole world” reflects Rome’s place as the empire’s hub, where news radiated across the Mediterranean, and in an honor-culture a community famed for faith carried real weight. Paul’s sense of being a “debtor” to Greeks and barbarians alike (v.14) — the standard division of humanity by language and culture — flows from his commission to the nations (v.5), and his hindered plans fit his hope to use Rome as a base for mission on to Spain (Romans 15:24).
8.christ: The gospel Paul is “ready to preach” (v.15) is the good news of God’s Son (v.3), and the very existence of a thriving church at the heart of the empire is testimony to the risen Lord gathering the nations to himself (cf. Acts 1:8). Paul’s whole drive westward is service to Christ’s universal reign — the mission to bring all peoples to the obedience of faith in him.

verses.note: “First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for you all, that your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world.” eastern Chrysostom notes the thanksgiving is offered “through Jesus Christ,” the Mediator through whom all praise ascends; puritan Matthew Henry marks the pastoral wisdom — Paul opens with praise of their faith, winning the heart and giving God the glory before he ever instructs.

Paul calls God to witness that he serves him “with my spirit in the gospel of his Son” and prays for the Romans unceasingly. grammatical-historical Robertson and reformed Calvin note the inward worship — not mere outward ceremony but the whole inner man engaged — and the solemn oath attests the sincerity of a love Paul bears a church he has never seen.

He prays that by the will of God he might at last have a prosperous journey to them. reformed Calvin marks the submission: Paul longs earnestly, yet lays the whole matter under God’s good pleasure. He would indeed reach Rome — though by a road he did not expect, as a prisoner (Acts 28) — a model of prayer that asks boldly yet yields wholly.

“For I long to see you, that I may impart unto you some spiritual gift, to the end ye may be established.” grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin read the gift as spiritual, not material — some grace or benefit for their edification — with their establishment in the faith as the aim.

At once Paul softens the claim: that we may be comforted together by the mutual faith both of you and me. reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Robertson admire the humility — lest he seem to assume superiority over a church not his own, he makes the benefit run both ways, the great apostle himself drawing comfort from the faith of ordinary believers.

“Oftentimes I purposed to come unto you, (but was let hitherto).” grammatical-historical Robertson notes that “let” carries the old English sense of hindered — Paul was prevented until now by various providences, his work pressing elsewhere (cf. Acts 16:6–7) — yet the purpose to bear fruit among them held firm.

I am debtor both to the Greeks, and to the Barbarians; both to the wise, and to the unwise. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson catch the force of “debtor”: having received the gospel and his commission, Paul owes its proclamation to all — and the two pairs together sweep in the whole race, none excluded from the obligation.

“So, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome also.” reformed Calvin hears the boldness: undaunted by the splendor and scorn of the imperial capital, Paul is eager to bring the despised gospel to the very seat of worldly power — the note of courage that breaks open into v.16.

witnesses.point: Paul begins with thanksgiving through Christ the Mediator, praising the Romans’ faith before he ventures to instruct them.
Wise and loving, Paul opens with praise that wins the heart and gives God the glory before any exhortation.
He tempers his offer to impart a gift into mutual encouragement, unwilling to assume superiority over a church not his own.
“Was let” means hindered in older English; Paul was repeatedly prevented from coming, and a “debtor” is one under real obligation to preach to all.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Frames Paul’s mission not as optional generosity but as a debt owed: having received the gospel, he is under obligation to all peoples, and his eagerness to preach even at Rome flows from that sense of obligation.

8.application: Paul joins genuine affection to theological purpose: he prays without ceasing, longs for fellowship, and seeks to give and receive rather than only to instruct. His “debt” to the nations reframes mission as obligation, not charity — the one who has been given Christ owes him to those who have not yet heard.

16.pericope_label: The Righteous Shall Live by Faith
16.exposition: Two verses now state the thesis of the whole letter. I am not ashamed of the gospel, Paul writes, “for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek.” The gospel is not advice but power — the very energy by which God saves. And it saves because therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith, sealed by the prophet’s word, “The just shall live by faith” (Habakkuk 2:4). Everything that follows — the universal indictment, justification, the life of the Spirit, Israel, and the transformed life — unfolds from this compact declaration. Its content is a righteousness that comes from God and is received by faith.
16.original_language: Two weighty terms anchor the thesis. The gospel is the dynamis (“power,” the word behind “dynamite”) of God — not information about salvation but the effective force that accomplishes it. And it reveals the dikaiosynē theou (“righteousness of God”), a phrase Paul will unfold for chapters: it can name God’s own righteous character, his saving activity, and the righteous standing he confers — most likely all at once. The much-debated ek pisteōs eis pistin (“from faith to faith,” v.17) underscores that faith is the beginning, middle, and end of the way of salvation.
16.historical_context: “I am not ashamed” (v.16) is a charged claim in Rome, where the gospel centered on a crucified man — and crucifixion was the most shameful of deaths, which Cicero said a Roman citizen should not even have to name. To proclaim a crucified Lord as Savior in the imperial capital defied the whole honor-and-shame calculus of Roman society. “To the Jew first, and also to the Greek” reflects the redemptive-historical order — Israel received the promises first — while the citation of Habakkuk 2:4 was a live text in the first century, read at Qumran of those faithful to the law, and by Paul of those who trust the Messiah.
16.christ: The “righteousness of God” revealed in the gospel (v.17) is disclosed in the death and resurrection of Christ, in whom God shows himself both just and the justifier of the believing (as Romans 3:21–26 will unfold). By making faith the sole means and Christ the sole ground, Paul concentrates the saving power of God in his Son: the gospel of which he is unashamed is the gospel of the crucified and risen Lord.

verses.note: “For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth.” grammatical-historical Barnes and puritan Matthew Henry note the daring — the cross was a stumbling-block to Jews and folly to Greeks, yet Paul glories in it. reformed Calvin presses “power” (dynamis): the gospel is no dead letter but the mighty agency by which God actually saves all who believe. “To the Jew first, and also to the Greek” marks the order of privilege, not a difference of terms (cf. Acts 13:46).

Here is the heart of the letter, and its great crux: therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith. The witnesses genuinely divide on the phrase “righteousness of God.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott take it as God’s method of justifying sinners — his way of accounting men righteous by faith, not his attribute of justice; reformed Gill and puritan Matthew Henry press the imputed righteousness of Christ, called “of God” by its author and approval; wesleyan-arminian Wesley embraces both. “From faith to faith” is read either as faith first to last (so Calvin and Robertson) or as the passage from Old-Testament to New-Testament faith (Henry, Clarke). All agree Paul grounds the whole on the prophet: the just shall live by faith.

witnesses.point: “The righteousness of God” is God’s appointed method of justifying men by faith — his way of treating sinners as righteous — rather than his own attribute of justice.
The gospel is the power of God, no dead letter but the saving agency itself; and “from faith to faith” means faith from first to last.
The righteousness revealed is the imputed righteousness of Christ, wrought by his obedience and death and reckoned to the believer.
Reads the phrase to embrace both the essential righteousness of God and the righteousness of faith he bestows on those who believe.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: Reads “the righteousness of God” chiefly as God’s own covenant faithfulness — the saving justice by which he keeps his promises and puts the world right — a construal debated against the older view that the phrase denotes mainly the justified status God imputes to the believer.

16.application: The gospel is “the power of God for salvation,” not merely a report about it: proclaimed, it effects what it announces. Its scope is leveling — “every one that believeth,” so that pedigree, status, and merit fall away before the single condition of faith. This is the text that would one day set the Reformation alight, and it remains the ground of Christian assurance: God himself supplies the righteousness he requires.

18.exposition: Against the righteousness just revealed Paul sets its dark counterpart. The wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness — the same present-tense “revealed” as in v.17, two simultaneous unveilings. What may be known of God is plain, for his eternal power and Godhead are clearly seen from the creation of the world, leaving all “without excuse.” Yet when they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were thankful; professing wisdom they became fools, and changed the glory of the incorruptible God into images of man and beast. The human problem, at its root, is idolatry — the willful suppression of a truth everyone has been given.
18.original_language: “Wrath” is orgē — not the flare of temper but God’s settled, holy opposition to sin, what has fitly been called “the wrath of reason and law.” Those who “hold” the truth do so by katechō (“hold down, suppress, restrain”), imprisoning what they know lest it check them. In v.20 the term is theiotēs (“divine nature, divinity”) rather than theotēs (“deity as person”) — the divine attributes legible in creation — and the verdict is anapologētos (“without excuse, without a defense”), courtroom language that frames creation-knowledge as accountability.
18.historical_context: Paul’s indictment of Gentile sin deliberately echoes the Hellenistic-Jewish argument of the Wisdom of Solomon, where idolatry is the fountain of all moral disorder — an argument his Jewish readers would applaud, before he turns it on them (Romans 2:1). “His eternal power and divine nature” meets Stoic and Platonic natural theology on its own ground: the divine can be inferred from the ordered cosmos. The language of exchange and darkened worship also retraces Israel’s own idolatry (cf. Psalms 106:20), universalizing the fall.
18.christ: The revelation of wrath against suppressed truth is the dark foil that makes the gospel good news: this is precisely the condition Christ’s atoning death answers (Romans 3:25). Humanity “changed the glory of the incorruptible God” for idols (v.23) and so falls short of that glory (Romans 3:23); Christ is the true image of God who restores fallen humanity to it. The God dimly known through creation has now made himself known definitively in his Son.

verses.note: “For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men.” grammatical-historical Robertson, Barnes, and Clarke are careful: God’s “wrath” is no human passion or revenge but his settled, holy displeasure against sin. Ungodliness offends against God, unrighteousness against men — the two tables together — and the guilty “hold down” the truth they possess, suppressing it lest it restrain them. Set beside v.17, wrath and righteousness are unveiled at once.

Their guilt rests on real knowledge: that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God hath shewed it unto them. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin agree there is a knowledge of God’s being and perfections available to all — implanted within and displayed without — though Calvin adds the careful limit: enough to leave men inexcusable, never enough of itself to save.

The invisible things of him… are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made — the unseen God discerned through the visible creation. grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note the precise term, his “eternal power and Godhead” (theiotēs, divine nature, not the fuller theotēs), so that men are “without excuse.” reformed Calvin draws out the design: the witness is clear enough that none can plead ignorance of their Maker.

The tragedy is not ignorance but ingratitude: when they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were thankful. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson locate the root sin here — the refusal to honor and thank the God they knew — from which the ruin flows: their reasonings “became vain,” and “their foolish heart was darkened.” reformed Calvin sees the empty speculations of the wise as the first fruit of that darkness.

“Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools.” grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson hear the oxymoron — a folly judicially inflicted, the wise made foolish. reformed Calvin universalizes it: the conceit is innate, for men everywhere try to be wise of themselves and drag God down to their own conception; the higher they rate their wisdom, the deeper their folly.

The descent reaches its floor: they changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things. grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott trace the descending scale of idolatry, from the human form down to birds, beasts, and reptiles; reformed Calvin names it a disgraceful bargain, the truth of God traded for a lie. eastern Theodoret marks the madness — men worshipping as gods the very creatures they eat, loathe, or kill (cf. Psalms 106:20).

witnesses.point: God’s wrath is his settled, holy displeasure against sin — the wrath of reason and law, not of passion; men “hold down” the truth they possess, while creation displays his theiotes, his divine nature.
Natural revelation is clear enough to leave every man without excuse, yet by our blindness it never suffices to save.
The idolatry of the nations is sheer madness — bowing as to gods before the very creatures men eat, loathe, or destroy.
The root of the ruin is ingratitude: knowing God, they refused to honor or thank him, and so their reasonings grew vain and their heart dark.

external.name: Thomas R. Schreiner
external.point: Argues that humanity’s fundamental sin is idolatry — the failure to honor and thank the God plainly revealed in creation — so that general revelation does not save but renders every person “without excuse” and accountable.

18.application: Paul locates the human predicament not first in particular vices but in a refusal to honor and thank God for what is plainly known (v.21). Sin is, at root, idolatry — exchanging the Creator for created things — and every other disorder flows from that misdirected worship. Because the truth is available to all and willfully suppressed, no one can finally plead ignorance.

24.exposition: Now Paul traces where suppressed worship leads, in a threefold judicial refrain: God gave them up (v.24, v.26, v.28). Because they changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped the creature more than the Creator, God handed them over — to uncleanness, to vile affections, and to a reprobate mind — issuing in a sprawling catalogue of vice: unrighteousness, envy, murder, deceit, faithlessness, mercilessness (vv.29–31). And at the last, though they know the judgment of God that such things deserve death, they “not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them.” The chapter ends with humanity entangled in the consequences of its own rebellion.
24.original_language: The refrain-verb is paredōken (“handed over, delivered up,” v.24), judicial language echoing God’s sentence in Psalms 81:12, “I gave them up unto their own hearts’ lust.” In v.28 Paul turns a deliberate wordplay: because they did not dokimazō (“see fit, approve”) to keep God in knowledge, God gave them to an adokimon (“disapproved, reprobate”) mind — the punishment precisely fitted to the crime. The “lie” of v.25 is idolatry itself, named a lie in the prophets’ own idiom.
24.historical_context: “God gave them up” is not bare permission but active handing-over, a courtroom sentence in which sin becomes its own punishment (cf. Psalms 81:12). Vice-lists like that of vv.29–31 were a familiar form in Greco-Roman moral philosophy, which Paul adapts to his own ends. And when he must voice the language of idolatry, he breaks off in the worshiping community’s reflex — “who is blessed for ever. Amen” — a doxology that recoils from the very blasphemy he describes.
24.christ: This unflinching diagnosis establishes the universal need only Christ can meet: a humanity “given over” cannot save itself. The deserved “death” sinners know to be just (v.32) is the very judgment Christ bore in their place (Romans 3:25). By painting the darkness so honestly, Paul magnifies the grace of the One who, “while we were yet sinners,” died for the ungodly (Romans 5:6) — the Savior who alone reverses the exchange that ruined the race.

verses.note: The first handing-over: Wherefore God also gave them up to uncleanness through the lusts of their own hearts. The witnesses divide on its force. grammatical-historical Robertson and eastern Theodoret read it as permission — God withdrew his restraint and left men to themselves, and the withdrawal sent them deeper down. reformed Calvin presses further: more than bare permission, God by his just judgment so orders that men are carried into sin by their own lusts — yet he is never the author of sin, whose root abides in the sinner. Either way the principle stands: sin is punished by sin (cf. Psalms 81:12).

The root named: they changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator. grammatical-historical Robertson and reformed Calvin note that “more than” means rather than, to the neglect of — the fundamental inversion of idolatry, the honor due the Creator given to the creature. grammatical-historical Ellicott hears Paul, shocked at the blasphemy he must record, turn aside to bless the God the idolaters robbed: “who is blessed for ever. Amen.”

The second handing-over follows from the first: for this cause God gave them up unto vile affections. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the logic — degrading passions are the practical fruit of a false worship. eastern Chrysostom presses the gravity, and notes that Paul deprives them of excuse: they “changed the natural use,” turning deliberately from what they knew.

The description continues, and with it the note of just recompense: they were receiving in themselves that recompence of their error which was meet. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson observe that Paul charges only what the Greek and Roman writers themselves openly attest; eastern Theodoret sees sin become its own penalty — a self-inflicted infamy heavier than any judge would impose.

The third handing-over turns on a pun: even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind. grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott, with reformed Calvin, catch the wordplay — because they did not approve to keep God, God gave them a disapproved mind, void of judgment. eastern Chrysostom notes the sin now lodged in the mind itself, the fount from which the flood of evils pours.

Out of that reprobate mind spills the catalogue: filled with all unrighteousness… full of envy, murder, debate, deceit, malignity; whisperers. eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical Robertson note the intensive force — “filled,” “all” — the heathen world saturated with sin. reformed Calvin observes that Paul lists the vices as they occurred, not in strict order: none was guilty of every one, yet none was free from all.

The list presses on: backbiters, haters of God, despiteful, proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents. grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson read “haters of God” as active enmity — the deepest root of the corruption — and note how the indictment descends from open slander to the invention of new evils and the breaking of the most natural bond, a child’s honor of a parent.

And it closes at the bottom: without understanding, covenantbreakers, without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the extremity of “without natural affection” — men sunk so low as to lose even the instinctive love of kindred; reformed Calvin sees in the whole list the abandoned mind left void of all sound judgment.

The climax, and the worst of it: who knowing the judgment of God… not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that their sin is against knowledge — conscience testified that such deeds deserve death — and reformed Calvin names the depth: to approve and delight in the sins of others is worse than to commit them, for passion might excuse the act, but settled pleasure in evil betrays a mind wholly given over. So the indictment of the Gentile world closes, every mouth stopped, and the trap is set to spring on the one who judges (Romans 2:1).

witnesses.point: The “giving up” is more than permission: by his just judgment God delivers men to their lusts, yet he is never the author of the sin whose root abides in them.
“Gave them over” is the withdrawal of God’s restraint; and the wordplay of v.28 — they rejected God, so God gave them a rejected mind — makes the punishment fit the crime.
The sin lodges in the mind itself; and to take pleasure in others’ sin is worse than to sin, the mark of a mind wholly abandoned to evil.
Their degradation is its own recompense — a self-inflicted infamy heavier than any judge could impose.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Stresses that “God gave them over” is God’s active judicial act, not mere permission: he consigns rebels to the consequences of their chosen idolatry, so that their sin itself becomes the present form of his wrath.

24.application: God’s wrath here is chiefly his handing sinners over to the consequences of their own rebellion — a sobering revelation that sin is its own deepening punishment, and that to be left to oneself is already a kind of judgment. The progression from idolatry to moral chaos warns that worship is never private: what we adore shapes how we live. And the final note — approving the evil others do — exposes a conscience so corrupted it applauds what it knows deserves death, the very depth from which the gospel must rescue.

_meta.title: The Gospel of God Concerning His Son
reflection: Paul says he is “not ashamed of the gospel,” because in it “the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith” (Romans 1:16–17). Where does the pull to be ashamed of the gospel touch you, and what changes when you take it to be God’s power, not merely his advice?

Paul diagnoses the human problem as idolatry — knowing God, people “glorified him not as God, neither were thankful” (Romans 1:21). What do you find yourself honoring and thanking in the place of God, and how does misdirected worship reshape the rest of a life?

Three times God’s wrath appears as his “giving them up” to what they wanted (Romans 1:24, v.26, v.28). What does it mean that judgment can look like getting exactly what we ask for? Where do you see that pattern at work?
